guide_section: D5 §5.3 — Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
stem: Halcyon Insights runs a multi-agent research system on the Claude Agent SDK: a coordinator delegates to four search subagents, each covering a different source type (news archive, SEC filings, academic index, internal wiki). During a market study, the filings subagent hits an expired OAuth token and the academic subagent's query legitimately matches zero papers. Both currently report the same thing to the coordinator: a bare string, "search unavailable". The coordinator reacts identically in both cases — it retries the subagent three times, then aborts the whole study. Retries can never fix the expired token, and the zero-match case needed no retry at all. Which error-propagation design lets the coordinator make intelligent recovery decisions?
anti_pattern: Subagents collapsing every failure into a generic error status ("search unavailable") that hides failure type, what was attempted, and partial results from the coordinator. The coordinator then cannot distinguish an access failure that needs credentials or rerouting from a valid empty result that needs no action, so it applies one blunt policy (blind retry, or full abort) to every situation.
choice_a: Wrap every subagent tool call in a uniform retry decorator (3 attempts, exponential backoff) and have subagents raise the same exception type on final failure, so the coordinator's error handling stays simple and consistent across all four subagents.
rationale_a: Uniform retries plus a uniform exception preserve the core problem: the coordinator still cannot tell an expired token (retry never helps; needs re-auth or rerouting) from a transient timeout (retry helps) from a valid empty result (nothing is wrong). Consistency of handling is not the goal — recovery-relevant information is.
choice_b: Have subagents return an empty result set on any failure so the pipeline always completes, and log the underlying errors to an observability channel that engineers review after the report ships.
rationale_b: This silently suppresses errors — the coordinator sees a successful empty query where there was actually an access failure, so the report ships with invisible coverage holes. Logging for later engineering review does not help the coordinator make a recovery decision during the run.
choice_c: Have each subagent return structured error context: the failure category (auth failure vs timeout vs valid empty result), the query it attempted, any partial results it collected before failing, and viable alternatives — so the coordinator can re-authenticate, reroute to another source, retry, or proceed while annotating the gap.
rationale_c: Correct. Structured error context (failure type, attempted query, partial results, alternatives) is exactly what enables intelligent coordinator recovery: expired token → surface for re-auth or reroute; timeout → targeted retry; valid empty result → proceed normally. The two currently-indistinguishable cases get correctly different treatment.
choice_d: Terminate the entire study immediately on the first subagent error and report it to the user, on the grounds that a report built from a degraded source set is worse than no report.
rationale_d: Terminating an entire workflow on a single subagent failure is the opposite anti-pattern to silent suppression: one legitimately empty academic query would kill a study whose other three sources succeeded. The guide calls out both blanket termination and silent suppression as anti-patterns; graceful degradation with annotated gaps is the goal.
notes: Guide D5 Task 5.3 knowledge: structured error context enables coordinator recovery; generic statuses hide context; suppression and full termination are both anti-patterns.

guide_section: D5 §5.3 — Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
stem: At Corvid Analytics, a coordinator agent fans research out to subagents that each make 20-40 tool calls against rate-limited external APIs. The current design propagates every tool error immediately: any timeout inside a subagent bubbles straight up to the coordinator, which kills that subagent and restarts it from scratch. Telemetry over a week shows that 85% of propagated errors were transient rate-limit timeouts that succeeded on a simple retry, and that restarted subagents redo an average of 30 tool calls' worth of completed work. Which division of error-handling responsibility fixes this?
anti_pattern: Subagents propagating every error — including transient, locally recoverable ones — up to the coordinator, which then discards completed work and restarts from zero. Local recovery belongs inside the subagent; the coordinator should only see errors the subagent genuinely cannot resolve, and those should arrive with partial results and a record of what was attempted so completed work is preserved.
choice_a: Keep immediate propagation but make coordinator restarts cheaper: snapshot each subagent's full message transcript on every tool call so a restarted subagent can be replayed from its last snapshot instead of from zero.
rationale_a: Transcript snapshotting treats the symptom (expensive restarts) while keeping the wrong division of responsibility: the coordinator is still interrupted for failures a one-line local retry would absorb, and replay machinery adds real complexity for the 85% of cases that never needed coordinator involvement at all.
choice_b: Have each subagent handle transient failures locally (retry with backoff, try an alternative source), and propagate only errors it cannot resolve — including in that report its partial results and what it already attempted, so the coordinator can decide next steps without redoing completed work.
rationale_b: Correct. This is the guide's split: local recovery within subagents for transient failures; propagation to the coordinator only for unresolvable errors, carrying partial results plus attempted approaches. The coordinator makes strategy decisions (reroute, degrade, re-scope) rather than re-executing work, and the 30-call redo cost disappears.
choice_c: Route every tool error to a dedicated error-triage subagent that classifies it and tells the coordinator whether to retry, so classification logic lives in one place instead of being duplicated across subagents.
rationale_c: An extra triage hop adds latency and a failure point to the 85% of errors a local retry resolves instantly, and the triage agent lacks the failing subagent's context (what was already tried, what partial results exist). Centralizing classification does not require centralizing recovery — transient retries are cheapest at the point of failure.
choice_d: Suppress errors inside subagents entirely: after one failed attempt, skip the failing call, return whatever was collected as a normal success, and let the synthesis step work with whatever arrives.
rationale_d: Skipping failures and reporting success silently converts access failures into invisible coverage gaps — the coordinator never learns a source was skipped, so it can neither reroute nor annotate the gap. Partial results are valuable, but they must arrive labeled as partial, alongside the unresolved error.
notes: Guide D5 Task 5.3 skill: local recovery for transient failures; propagate only unresolvable errors with partial results and attempted approaches.

guide_section: D5 §5.3 — Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
stem: Bracken Research's multi-agent pipeline synthesizes weekly competitive briefs from five source families. This week two of them are down hard: a vendor API is mid-migration (returns 503 for at least 48 hours) and a paywalled archive rejected the renewed contract credentials. The synthesis subagent still produced a coherent brief from the three healthy sources. The team is split: one engineer wants to ship the brief as-is since it reads complete; another wants the coordinator to fail the entire run because a brief missing two source families is misleading. What should the pipeline do?
anti_pattern: Treating degraded-coverage synthesis as all-or-nothing: either shipping output that silently reads as complete (readers can't tell findings-from-three-sources from findings-from-five) or aborting the entire workflow because some sources failed. Both discard the correct middle path — deliver the work that succeeded, with explicit coverage annotations marking which findings are well-supported and which topic areas have gaps from unavailable sources.
choice_a: Ship the brief as-is: three of five source families is a majority, the text is coherent, and flagging infrastructure problems in a client-facing document undermines confidence in the research team.
rationale_a: Shipping without disclosure silently suppresses the coverage gap — a reader cannot distinguish 'no pricing movement happened' from 'the sources that would have shown pricing movement were unreachable'. Coherent prose is not evidence of complete coverage; this is the silent-suppression anti-pattern at the report level.
choice_b: Fail the whole run and deliver nothing until all five source families are healthy again, since a brief built on partial coverage could mislead decisions.
rationale_b: Aborting the entire workflow over partial source failure throws away three source families' worth of valid, timely findings — and with a 48-hour vendor migration, guarantees the weekly brief simply doesn't exist. Blanket termination on partial failure is an anti-pattern; the fix for 'could mislead' is explicit gap annotation, not non-delivery.
choice_c: Have the coordinator keep retrying the two failed sources in a loop and hold the brief until they recover, so the team never has to choose between completeness and delivery.
rationale_c: Retry cannot fix either failure mode — a 48-hour migration outage and rejected credentials are not transient — so this is an unbounded wait that quietly becomes option B (no delivery) while burning API budget on hopeless retries. Retries are for transient failures; durable failures need degradation decisions.
choice_d: Deliver the brief with structured coverage annotations: mark which findings are well-supported by the healthy sources, list which topic areas have gaps because the filings vendor and paywalled archive were unavailable, and note when full coverage is expected to resume.
rationale_d: Correct. The guide's target skill is structuring synthesis output with coverage annotations — distinguishing well-supported findings from topic areas with gaps caused by unavailable sources. Readers get the valid findings now, plus exactly the information needed to weigh what's missing; neither silent suppression nor full termination.
notes: Guide D5 Task 5.3 skill: synthesis output with coverage annotations indicating well-supported findings vs gap areas.

guide_section: D1 §1.7 — Manage session state, resumption, and forking
stem: A platform engineer at Vantage Systems spends 40 minutes and ~200K tokens of tool calls having a Claude Agent SDK session build a deep analysis of their monolith's data layer: table ownership, transaction boundaries, and every call site of the shared ORM helpers. Now she wants to evaluate two mutually exclusive refactoring strategies — strangler-fig extraction vs in-place module boundaries — and needs each strategy explored against that full analysis, independently, so that conclusions drawn while exploring one strategy don't color the exploration of the other. What is the right session-management approach?
anti_pattern: Re-running expensive analysis from scratch (or letting two alternative explorations contaminate each other in one continued session) because the architecture ignores session forking. fork_session exists precisely to branch independent explorations off a shared, already-paid-for analysis baseline: each fork inherits the full prior context, and neither fork sees the other's reasoning.
choice_a: Fork the completed analysis session twice with fork_session, run the strangler-fig exploration in one fork and the module-boundary exploration in the other, and compare the two forks' conclusions.
rationale_a: Correct. fork_session creates independent branches from a shared baseline: both forks inherit the complete 200K-token analysis without re-running it, and each exploration proceeds in isolation, so neither strategy's reasoning contaminates the other. This is the guide's canonical fork use case — comparing divergent approaches from one shared analysis.
choice_b: Continue in the same session sequentially: explore strangler-fig first, then tell the agent to 'set aside everything you just concluded' and explore module boundaries with fresh eyes in the following turns.
rationale_b: A single session cannot un-see its own conclusions — the strangler-fig exploration stays in the conversation history and biases the second exploration no matter what the prompt claims. An instruction to disregard prior reasoning is prompt-level wishful thinking where the requirement was structural isolation.
choice_c: Start two brand-new sessions, one per strategy, and have each session redo the data-layer analysis itself so that each strategy is evaluated against analysis it performed and therefore fully trusts.
rationale_c: This pays the 40-minute / 200K-token analysis cost twice for no isolation benefit that forking doesn't already provide — and the two independent re-analyses may diverge from each other, making the strategy comparison confounded by analysis drift rather than clean.
choice_d: Ask the current session for a one-paragraph summary of the analysis, then paste that paragraph into two new sessions as their starting context, keeping both lightweight.
rationale_d: Starting fresh with an injected summary is the right pattern when prior context is stale or bloated — but here the analysis is current, and a one-paragraph summary discards the call-site-level detail (specific tables, transaction boundaries, helper call sites) that the strategy evaluations must reason over. Forking preserves that full baseline for free.
notes: Guide D1 Task 1.7 skill: fork_session for parallel exploration branches from a shared codebase analysis.

guide_section: D1 §1.7 — Manage session state, resumption, and forking
stem: On Monday, an engineer at Fernline ran a named Claude Code investigation session over a payment service and ended the day mid-task; the session's context holds detailed analysis of about 30 files. Overnight, teammates merged two PRs that rewrote 3 of those files (renamed functions, moved a retry helper) and left the other 27 untouched. On Tuesday she runs `claude --resume payments-investigation` to continue. The prior context is largely still valid, but the agent's knowledge of those 3 files is now wrong, and she's seen resumed sessions confidently reference stale line numbers before. What is the best way to continue?
anti_pattern: Resuming a session after the codebase changed without telling the agent what changed — so it acts on stale analysis (wrong line numbers, renamed functions) — or overcorrecting by discarding a mostly-valid session and re-exploring everything. A resumed session should be told exactly which files changed so it re-reads only those, keeping the 90% of prior analysis that is still good.
choice_a: Resume and continue the task directly — the agent will notice discrepancies itself when its edits fail to apply, and self-correct by re-reading whatever files it needs at that point.
rationale_a: Relying on edit failures to surface staleness is reactive and unreliable: the agent may reason from stale analysis in ways that never trigger a hard failure (e.g., recommending changes to a function that was renamed and now has different callers), producing confidently wrong output rather than a clean error.
choice_b: Resume the named session and open with a message listing the two merged PRs and the 3 rewritten files, asking the agent to re-read those specific files before continuing — keeping the rest of its analysis as-is.
rationale_b: Correct. The guide's skill is exactly this: inform a resumed session about specific file changes for targeted re-analysis rather than full re-exploration. The 27 untouched files' analysis remains valid and paid-for; the 3 changed files get re-read; stale-line-number errors are preempted at the cost of three file reads.
choice_c: Abandon the session and start fresh: any post-resume divergence between context and code is unrecoverable, so re-run the full 30-file investigation from zero to guarantee consistency.
rationale_c: Full re-exploration buys consistency at maximum cost when 27 of 30 files are unchanged. Starting fresh (ideally with an injected summary) is the right call when prior context is mostly stale — here it is mostly valid, which is precisely the case session resumption exists for.
choice_d: Resume, but instruct the agent to treat all prior tool results as untrusted and re-verify every file before every edit for the rest of the session, guaranteeing it never acts on stale data.
rationale_d: Blanket re-verification of all 30 files on every action turns the resumed session's main asset — its accumulated analysis — into dead weight and multiplies token cost and latency across the whole session. The engineer knows exactly which 3 files changed; targeted re-analysis uses that knowledge, blanket distrust wastes it.
notes: Guide D1 Task 1.7 skill: informing a resumed session about specific file changes for targeted re-analysis rather than full re-exploration.

guide_section: D3 §3.5 — Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
stem: A team at Quarrel Labs is using Claude Code to build a parser for a quirky in-house log format (mixed timestamp styles, optional continuation lines, escaped delimiters). Their loop so far: describe the format in prose, get an implementation, spot a broken edge case by eyeballing output, describe the fix in more prose, repeat. Each round fixes the described case and quietly regresses a previously working one, because nothing pins the already-correct behavior down. Version 6 just broke continuation-line handling that worked in version 4. What refinement technique breaks this cycle?
anti_pattern: Iterating on generated code purely through prose descriptions of desired behavior, with no executable specification pinning down what already works — so each fix regresses earlier behavior invisibly, and 'done' is whenever eyeballing stops finding problems. Test-driven iteration inverts this: the test suite defines done, and failures are shared back as the concrete, machine-checkable improvement signal.
choice_a: Write a much more rigorous prose specification — enumerate every timestamp style, continuation rule, and escape case in a single detailed document — and have Claude Code regenerate the parser from that spec in one shot.
rationale_a: A better spec helps the next generation but doesn't fix the loop: prose is still interpreted, not executed, so nothing detects when round 7 silently regresses a rule from the spec's middle. The failure mode was never missing detail — it was the absence of an executable check that pins working behavior down between rounds.
choice_b: Ask Claude Code to review its own parser after each round and list edge cases it might have broken, fixing whatever it identifies before the next iteration.
rationale_b: Self-review inherits the generator's blind spots — the model retains the reasoning that produced the bug, and its list of 'might have broken' cases is a guess, not a measurement. Regression detection needs execution against fixed expectations, not the author's introspection.
choice_c: Have Claude Code first write a test suite covering the expected behavior and known edge cases (timestamp styles, continuation lines, escaped delimiters), then iterate on the parser by running the suite and sharing the specific test failures each round until all pass.
rationale_c: Correct. This is the guide's test-driven iteration skill: the suite freezes already-correct behavior so regressions surface immediately as named failures, and sharing those failures gives the model a concrete, unambiguous target each round — version 6 breaking continuation lines would have been caught the moment it happened, not two versions later.
choice_d: Keep the current loop but have a second, independent Claude instance review each new parser version before adoption, since a reviewer without the generator's context catches subtler issues than the author.
rationale_d: Independent review is a real technique (the guide covers it for review architectures), but it's still probabilistic reading of code, not execution — a reviewer can miss a continuation-line regression that a failing test states outright. For iterative refinement of testable behavior, the executable suite is the primary loop; review complements it.
notes: Guide D3 Task 3.5 knowledge/skill: test-driven iteration — write suites covering expected behavior and edge cases first, then iterate by sharing test failures.

guide_section: D3 §3.5 — Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
stem: An infrastructure engineer at Moorgate is about to have Claude Code implement a caching layer in front of a rate-limited vendor API, a domain she hasn't worked in before. Past attempts at similar tasks produced code that was structurally fine but wrong about things nobody wrote down: which endpoints tolerate stale reads, what happens to in-flight requests when credentials rotate, how cache failures should degrade. Those requirements existed — in her head and her team's runbooks — but never made it into the prompt, and each gap was discovered only after implementation. Which technique addresses this failure mode directly at the start of the task?
anti_pattern: Prompting an implementation task in an unfamiliar or under-specified domain as a one-way instruction, so requirements that live in the developer's head (invalidation policy, failure-mode behavior, degradation strategy) never enter the conversation and surface only as post-implementation defects. The interview pattern flips the direction: Claude asks questions first, surfacing considerations the developer didn't think to state.
choice_a: Paste the vendor API docs, the team's runbooks, and several existing caching implementations into context before the implementation prompt, so the answers to any question Claude might have are already available somewhere in the material.
rationale_a: Bulk-loading reference material makes answers findable but doesn't make the questions get asked — the model has no signal about which of the hundreds of facts in the runbooks are the load-bearing requirements for this task, and stale-read tolerance per endpoint may appear nowhere in writing. The gap was elicitation, not access.
choice_b: Before any implementation, have Claude Code interview her: ask it to pose the questions it needs answered about invalidation, staleness tolerance, credential rotation, and failure degradation, and only start implementing once she's answered them.
rationale_b: Correct. This is the guide's interview pattern: having Claude ask questions first surfaces design considerations the developer may not have anticipated stating — exactly the failure mode described, where requirements existed in heads and runbooks but never entered the prompt. The Q&A becomes explicit shared context before code is written.
choice_c: Use plan mode: have Claude Code explore the codebase and produce an implementation plan for approval before writing code, catching design problems at the plan stage instead of in the diff.
rationale_c: Plan mode surfaces what's discoverable in the codebase and sequences the work, but the missing requirements here aren't in the codebase — stale-read tolerance and rotation behavior live in the engineer's head and ops lore. A plan built on the same silent assumptions just documents them earlier; the interview pattern is what pulls unstated requirements out.
choice_d: Implement in small increments with a review checkpoint after each slice, so any wrong assumption is caught within one slice rather than after the whole layer is built.
rationale_d: Incremental review shrinks the blast radius of each wrong assumption but still discovers requirements by hitting them — the credential-rotation behavior gets corrected only after a slice embodies the wrong guess. Elicitation up front is strictly cheaper than detection after the fact for requirements someone can simply state when asked.
notes: Guide D3 Task 3.5 knowledge/skill: the interview pattern — Claude asks questions to surface considerations (cache invalidation, failure modes) before implementing in unfamiliar domains.

guide_section: D1 §1.4 — Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
stem: Brindle Retail's support-resolution agent (Claude Agent SDK, MCP tools: get_customer, lookup_order, process_refund, escalate_to_human) has a compliance rule: no refund may be processed until the customer's identity has been verified via get_customer. The system prompt states this mandate in bold, twice. Production audit over a month: in 6% of refund conversations the agent called process_refund without ever calling get_customer — typically when the customer pasted an order number and full account details up front, making verification feel redundant in-context. Compliance requires this rate to be exactly zero. What change achieves that?
anti_pattern: Enforcing a hard workflow-ordering requirement (identity verification before financial operations) through prompt instructions alone. Prompt mandates are probabilistic — persuasive context (customer volunteering details) produces a nonzero skip rate — so deterministic compliance requires a programmatic prerequisite gate in the tool layer that makes the violating call structurally impossible, not merely discouraged.
choice_a: Escalate the prompt: move the verification mandate to the first line of the system prompt, phrase it as an absolute imperative with consequences, and repeat it in the process_refund tool description.
rationale_a: Louder placement and repetition shift probabilities but keep enforcement probabilistic — the documented failure mode (customer-volunteered details making verification feel redundant) is exactly the persuasive context that overrides instructions occasionally. A compliance target of exactly zero cannot be met by any prompt wording.
choice_b: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt showing the agent calling get_customer first even when the customer volunteers complete account details, so the ambiguous case that drives the skips is explicitly demonstrated.
rationale_b: Few-shot examples are the right tool for shaping judgment in ambiguous cases, and would likely shrink the 6% — but shaping is still sampling, not enforcement. For a rule where the tolerated violation rate is zero, demonstration reduces risk; only a programmatic gate eliminates it.
choice_c: Leave generation unchanged and add a nightly audit job that scans the day's tool-call logs for refunds lacking a prior get_customer call, automatically reversing those refunds and flagging the conversations for review.
rationale_c: Post-hoc detection concedes the violation: the non-compliant refund executes, money moves, and the customer experiences a reversal — auditing catches it after the fact rather than preventing it. Detection complements enforcement but cannot substitute for it when the requirement is that the violating action never occur.
choice_d: Add a programmatic prerequisite in the tool layer: a hook (or gate in the process_refund handler) that blocks any process_refund call unless get_customer has already returned a verified customer ID in this conversation, returning a structured error that redirects the agent to verify first.
rationale_d: Correct. This is the guide's programmatic-enforcement skill for workflow ordering: when deterministic compliance is required, prompt instructions have a non-zero failure rate by nature, and a prerequisite gate that structurally blocks the downstream call until the prerequisite has completed is the only mechanism that yields exactly zero. The structured error also steers the agent into the correct sequence mid-conversation.
notes: Guide D1 Task 1.4 knowledge/skill: programmatic prerequisites blocking downstream tool calls (block process_refund until get_customer returns a verified ID); prompt-alone ordering has non-zero failure rate.
